natural_occurrence.description: Synthetic antioxidant created by esterifying gallic acid (which occurs naturally in tea, oak bark, witch hazel) with propyl alcohol. Used in food industry since 1948.

manufacturing.description: Produced by esterifying gallic acid (3,4,5-trihydroxybenzoic acid) with propanol (propyl alcohol). The resulting compound is a white to creamy-white crystalline powder. Linear formula: 3,4,5-(HO)₃C₆H₂CO₂CH₂CH₂CH₃, Molecular weight: 212.20.

eu.conditions: E311 (octyl gallate) and E312 (dodecyl gallate) removed from EU list in 2018; E310 remains with restrictions

usa.notes: FDA allows <0.02% of fat/oil content (individually or combined with octyl/dodecyl gallate, BHT, BHA, ascorbyl palmitate)

japan.notes: Used particularly in butter and oil products with defined limits

regulatory_history.event: SCF establishes group ADI of 0-0.2 mg/kg bw for three gallates (E310, E311, E312)
JECFA allocates ADI only to propyl gallate; does not establish ADIs for octyl and dodecyl gallate
EFSA re-evaluation establishes ADI of 0.5 mg/kg bw/day for propyl gallate
EU removes E311 (octyl gallate) and E312 (dodecyl gallate) from approved list; E310 remains approved

adi_context.source_note: Based on EFSA ADI of 0.5 mg/kg bw/day established in 2014. JECFA set a higher ADI of 0-1.4 mg/kg bw/day in 1996.

fun_facts.title: Vintage Antioxidant
fun_facts.description: One of the longest-established synthetic antioxidants in food use, protecting our fats and oils since 1948 - over 75 years of service!

fun_facts.title: Synergy Superstar
fun_facts.description: Works synergistically when combined with citric acid, BHA, or BHT, creating antioxidant combinations more powerful than any single ingredient alone.

fun_facts.title: Free Radical Terminator
fun_facts.description: Donates hydrogen atoms or electrons to neutralize free radicals before they can attack fatty acid chains and trigger the oxidation cascade - stopping rancidity at its source.

fun_facts.title: The Survivor
fun_facts.description: While its cousins octyl gallate (E311) and dodecyl gallate (E312) were removed from EU's approved list in 2018 due to safety concerns, propyl gallate continues to be approved and used.

fun_facts.title: Gallic Acid Origins
fun_facts.description: Derived from gallic acid, a compound naturally found in tea leaves, oak bark, and witch hazel - the synthetic propyl ester extends the natural compound's usefulness.
